title: Education Data Workflow Desk Reference
text:
Preface
This book exists to get you productive in R quickly, using the kind of data you already work with every day. It’s not a general-purpose “learn programming” course — it’s written specifically for people who do district or state-level assessment and accountability work, currently in Excel, SAS, Access, or some mix of all three.

title: Education Data Workflow Desk Reference
text:
What this book assumes

You’re comfortable with spreadsheets, pivot tables, and probably some SQL or VLOOKUP-style logic — you understand the concepts of filtering, sorting, joining, and summarizing data, even if you’ve never written them in code.
You have never written R before. That’s the whole point.
You want to be doing real, useful work within days, not after a semester of coursework.

title: Education Data Workflow Desk Reference
section: What makes this different from a generic R tutorial
text:
What makes this different from a generic R tutorial
Every example in this book uses the same dataset: a synthetic version of a Department of Education Static File — student-level enrollment and assessment data, the kind of file that sits at the center of A–F accountability work. You’ll work with the same columns, the same kinds of messy real-world values (missing data, inconsistent grade levels, multiple records per student), and the same sorts of questions (pass rates by school, growth categories, subgroup counts) that you’d typically encounter in your work.
By the time you finish the core chapters, you won’t just know that group_by() exists — you’ll have used it to calculate a real pass rate, by real school, on real-shaped data.

title: Education Data Workflow Desk Reference
text:
How to use this book
Read Part 1 and Part 2 in order — they’re short, and they build the foundation everything else depends on. After that, treat the rest as a reference: jump to whatever chapter matches the task in front of you. The navigation links are designed to let you find your way back without scrolling.

title: Education Data Workflow Desk Reference
text:
The dataset
A sample of the synthetic Static File (static_file_sample.csv) accompanies this book, along with its codebook describing every column. You’ll first meet it in Your First Import, and you’ll use it throughout. The full codebook is worth keeping open in a separate tab — column meanings in the data are not always intuitive from the name alone, and this book will repeatedly point you back to it.

title: 1  Why R First
text:
1.1 What Python is genuinely better at
If you’re going to invest real time learning a programming language for education data work, the honest question is: why R, and not Python — especially when Python is more general-purpose and shows up more often in broader data science and machine learning conversations?
It’s worth being upfront about this rather than pretending the choice is obvious. Python tends to win on:
If your work were mostly software engineering, Python would likely be the better starting point. But that’s not most assessment and accountability work.

title: 1  Why R First
text:
Stability and interoperability — it’s the default language of choice in most general software engineering contexts, and integrates more easily into production systems, web applications, and APIs outside of data analysis.
General-purpose use — Python is a better choice if you’re building something that isn’t primarily about analyzing a dataset (an application, a scheduled automation system, a web scraper).
Machine learning and AI tooling — the dominant ML/AI libraries (PyTorch, TensorFlow, most LLM tooling) are Python-first, with R support often secondary or absent.

title: 1  Why R First
section: 1.2 What R is genuinely better at, for this work specifically
text:
1.2 What R is genuinely better at, for this work specifically

It works out of the box for data analysis. Reading a file, summarizing it, and making a publication-quality chart takes fewer lines and less setup friction in R than in Python, where you’re more often assembling several libraries yourself.
A more controlled, uniform ecosystem. The “tidyverse” family of packages (which you’ll use constantly in this book) was designed by a small, coordinated team to work together consistently — function names, argument order, and output formats follow shared conventions. Python’s data ecosystem is powerful but more fragmented, with more ways to do the same thing.
Visualization and presentation. R’s ggplot2 (and the broader “grammar of graphics” approach you’ll learn in Visualization) remains one of the most expressive and widely respected charting tools in any language, and R’s document-generation tooling (Quarto, which this entire book is built with) makes producing polished reports and slides remarkably straightforward.

title: 1  Why R First
section: 1.3 The most important reason: a shared language with your field
text:
1.3 The most important reason: a shared language with your field
Here’s the argument that matters most for your day-to-day work: there is a growing, active community in education data and accountability that works in R, and that shared language is very valuable.
A few concrete examples:

The SGP package — used to calculate Student Growth Percentiles, a core concept in state accountability systems — is written in R. If your state’s growth model uses SGP methodology, you may already work with some R code and data.
Researchers are actively building suites of tools that can simplify or perform common tasks. For example, Nathan Dadey’s ReportR package works directly with the same data format used to produce Student Growth Percentile calculations and related calculations. You can import your file, and create useful reports and analytics. Tools built by and for people doing exactly your kind of work are shareable and adaptable in R.
When you hit a wall, there may be an analyst who’s solved your exact problem in R, in a forum post, a GitHub repo.
Finally, since your analysis will exist fully as text, any part of the process can be copy/pasted to an AI tool; with many education researchers working in R, you can often receive answers very tailored to your needs.

Learning the language your professional community already speaks can reduce the time you spend troubleshooting.

title: 1  Why R First
section: 1.4 You’re not choosing a side forever
text:
1.4 You’re not choosing a side forever
This isn’t a permanent fork in the road. There’s a growing “polyglot” movement actively working to align R and Python rather than treat them as competitors. Wes McKinney, co-creator of Python’s pandas, and Hadley Wickham, creator of R’s tidyverse — two people who have each individually shaped the modern data science landscape — both work at the same nonprofit, Posit, building tools designed to let the two languages work together and share strengths (Arrow and Parquet, which you’ll encounter in Database Connections, are good examples of this convergence in practice).
Practically, this means: nearly everything you learn here — thinking in data frames, vectorized operations, the logic of filtering and grouping and joining — transfers directly if you ever do pick up Python later. You’re not learning a dead-end skill. You’re learning the dialect your field already speaks, with a clear bridge to learn Python if and when you need it.

title: 2  Initial Setup
text:
2.1 Install R
This chapter gets you from nothing installed to a working R environment. Do this once; you won’t revisit most of it.
R itself is the underlying language and engine — separate from any editor or interface you’ll use to write it. Download it from the Comprehensive R Archive Network (CRAN):
You won’t interact with R directly very often after this — almost everything you do will happen inside Positron, covered next. Installing R is simply making the engine available for Positron to use.

title: 2  Initial Setup
text: Windows / Mac: https://cran.r-project.org, choose your platform, run the installer with default settings.

title: 2  Initial Setup
text:
2.2 Install Positron
Positron is the editor (technically, the IDE — integrated development environment) you’ll actually work in day to day. More on why it’s a strong choice in Meet Positron; for now, just get it installed:

Download from https://positron.posit.co
Run the installer with default settings
Open Positron once after installing to confirm it launches correctly

title: 2  Initial Setup
text:
2.3 Install packages
A package is a bundle of pre-written functions someone else built and shared — conceptually similar to an Excel add-in, except the available universe is enormous (CRAN alone hosts over 20,000 packages).
Open Positron, find the Console pane (bottom-left by default), and run:

install.packages("tidyverse")

This single command installs a whole family of packages that work together consistently — dplyr, ggplot2, readr, tidyr, and more — covering the large majority of what this book teaches. It can take a minute or two; that’s normal.
A few additional packages you’ll want for specific chapters later in this book, worth installing now while you’re at it:

install.packages(c("readxl", "writexl", "janitor", "googlesheets4",
                    "DBI", "odbc", "duckdb", "arrow",
                    "gt", "DT", "plotly", "glue", "skimr"))

Installing is a one-time action per machine. You won’t run install.packages() again for these unless you switch computers or need a newer version later.

title: 2  Initial Setup
text:
2.4 Load libraries
Installing a package puts it on your machine. Loading a package makes it available in your current R session — you do this every time you start working, not just once:

library(tidyverse)

A simple way to think about the difference: install.packages() is like buying a tool and putting it in your garage. library() is like carrying that tool into the room you’re working in today. You only need to buy it once, but you need to bring it into the room each time you want to use it.

title: 2  Initial Setup
text:
2.5 A new file
You’ll work primarily in two file types throughout this book:

A new Quarto document (.qmd) — combines narrative text and executable code chunks together, and is what produces the reports, slides, and reference documents this book itself is built from. Covered in more depth in Quarto Essentials.
A new R file (.R) — a plain script, just code, no prose. Better suited to automation tasks meant to run unattended (like the example in Project Walkthrough 1).

In Positron, both are created the same way: File → New File, then choose the type, or use the icon in the top toolbar.

title: 2  Initial Setup
text:
2.6 Start an R session
Before you can run any code, Positron needs an active R session — the actual running R process your code executes against. Positron will often start one automatically when you open a .R or .qmd file, but if you ever see no console activity or an error about no interpreter being found, you can start one manually from the interpreter picker in the top status bar (see Meet Positron for exactly where this lives).

With R, Positron, and your core packages installed, you’re ready to open your first real file — which is exactly what the next chapter does.

title: 3  Meet Positron
text:
3.1 Why Positron
Positron is a relatively new IDE built specifically for data science work, and it’s a strong starting point for this book for a few concrete reasons:

title: 3  Meet Positron
text:
It’s data-science-first, not adapted from a general-purpose code editor after the fact — the layout and built-in panes are designed around the rhythm of working with data, looking at tables, and viewing visualizations.
It’s R and Python compatible. If you ever do pick up Python later (see the closing note in Why R First), you don’t need to learn an entirely new editor — Positron handles both natively.
It’s actively developed and current, built and maintained by Posit, the same organization behind much of the tidyverse and Quarto tooling this book relies on throughout.
It will feel familiar if you’ve used VS Code. Positron is built on the same underlying editor framework as Visual Studio Code, one of the most widely used code editors across all of software development. If you’ve ever poked around VS Code for any reason, a lot of Positron’s layout, shortcuts, and extension model will already feel recognizable.

title: 3  Meet Positron
text:
3.2 Positron basics
When you open Positron, you’ll generally see four areas worth knowing by name, since the rest of this book will refer to them directly:
Script editor — the main pane, usually centered or top-left, where you write and edit your .R or .qmd files. This is where your actual code and prose live.

Console — typically bottom-left. This is where code actually executes and where you’ll see printed output, errors, and messages. You can type directly into the console for quick one-off checks, but most of your work will be written in the script editor and sent to the console to run.

Variables pane — typically top-right. Shows every object currently loaded in your R session: every data frame, vector, or value you’ve created, along with its type and a quick preview. This is a very useful tool for examining your data sets.

From the variable pane, you can also open your data sets to interact with them like viewing, filtering, and sorting a spreadsheet. After performing the desired changes, you can click a button to export your actions as code. There is also a helpful sidebar that shows summary visuals and statistics for each column.

Viewer — typically bottom-right. This is where rendered output shows up: data frame previews in table form, plots, and rendered Quarto documents when you preview them. The viewer has a pop out button to let you see output larger in a web browser.

You don’t need to memorize exact pane positions — Positron’s layout is adjustable, and the exact arrangement can vary by version. What matters is recognizing these four roles when you see them.

title: 3  Meet Positron
text:
3.3 Running code
To run a line (or selected block) of code from the script editor into the console:

Windows: Ctrl + Enter
Mac: Cmd + Enter
Run entire chunk Shift + Enter

These are the most-used keyboard shortcuts. Write a line in your script, press the shortcut, watch it execute in the console below.

title: 3  Meet Positron
text:
3.4 Checking your R interpreter
Positron needs to know which R installation to actually run your code against — this matters more once you’re juggling multiple projects or package environments, but it’s worth knowing where to look from day one. Look in the top status bar of the Positron window for an interpreter indicator (it will typically show an R version number). Clicking it lets you select a different interpreter if you have more than one R installation, or restart the current session if something gets into a broken state.

If your code won’t run and the console shows no response at all, this is the first place to check — it usually means no interpreter is currently selected or running.
With the basics of the interface in hand, it’s time to actually open a real file — let’s start with your first import.

title: 4  Your First Import
text:
4.1 The dataset you’ll use throughout this book
Let’s skip the abstract examples. You’re going to load a real file and look at it, right now.
Every example from here forward uses the same file: a synthetic, realistic version of an Arizona Department of Education Static File — the kind of student-level enrollment and assessment file that sits at the center of A–F accountability calculations. It has 80 columns and tens of thousands of student records across 30 schools, with the same structure, the same messiness, and the same kinds of questions you’d actually be asked to answer in real accountability work.
Alongside the data file, you have a codebook — a separate reference describing exactly what every column means and what values it can take. Open it now and skim it; you’ll come back to it constantly. Column names like FAY, SGP_CCR, or ELFEP are not self-explanatory on first glance, and that’s normal — this is real accountability terminology, not something you’re expected to intuit.
crumbs: Your First Data, Then the Concepts Behind It

title: 4  Your First Import
text:
4.2 Loading the file

library(tidyverse)

static_file <- read_csv("data/static_file_sample.csv")

That’s it. One function call, and 35,000+ rows of student data are now sitting in an object called static_file, ready to work with.
A couple of things worth noticing immediately:

read_csv() is the tidyverse’s function for reading comma-separated files — faster and more predictable than base R’s older read.csv().
The arrow (<-) is how you assign something a name in R. Read static_file <- read_csv(...) as “take the result of reading this file, and call it static_file.” You’ll use <- constantly; it’s R’s equivalent of typing a value into a named cell and having it stick.
Nothing printed to your screen. Unlike opening a file in Excel, reading data into R doesn’t automatically show it to you — it just loads it into memory under the name you gave it. Looking at it is a separate, deliberate step, which is exactly what comes next.
crumbs: Your First Data, Then the Concepts Behind It

title: 4  Your First Import
section: 4.3 Actually looking at what you just loaded
text:

glimpse(static_file)

glimpse() gives you a compact, column-by-column preview — every column name, its data type, and the first several values, oriented so you can scan 80 columns without horizontal scrolling. This is usually the very first thing you’ll run after loading any new file.
A few more orientation commands worth running right away, especially on a file this wide:

nrow(static_file)       # how many rows (records)?

[1] 35560

ncol(static_file)        # how many columns?

[1] 80

n_distinct(static_file$SAISID)   # how many *unique students* — not the same as rows!

[1] 17462


That last one matters more than it might look. This file has far more rows than unique students — a single student can appear multiple times (different subjects tested, multiple years of EL evaluation history, and so on). Run that line and you’ll see the gap directly. This is your first real lesson in thinking about education data: a row is a record, not necessarily a person, and knowing the difference is the kind of thing that quietly breaks an analysis if you don’t check it up front.
If you want a fuller statistical snapshot of every column at once — not just types, but distributions, missing-value counts, and quick summary stats — the skimr package (installed back in Initial Setup) is worth knowing about:

library(skimr)
skim(static_file)

title: 4  Your First Import
section: 4.4 What you just did, in plain terms
text:
4.4 What you just did, in plain terms
You loaded a file, and asked four key questions: what’s in here, how big is it, how many of these rows represent the same person, and what does each column generally look like? That’s probably a similar process to you what you already do when opening any new data set, but you just did it as two or three lines of code instead of a sequence of clicks. That creates a record of exactly what you did, so the process can be saved, repeated, and eventually automated.
This really pays off when you have to repeat a process. I’ve had workflows in spreadsheet where I had to write down a lengthy set of instructions to make sure I reproduced the same steps in each tab. Sometimes I could record a macro, but if I missed a step, or found a mistake, it could be problematic to undo all the actions. When working in R, you don’t alter your original file, and you can quickly and easily alter steps and simply re-run the code.
The next chapter gives more details on the foundational pieces you just used — data frames, vectors, etc. We’ll continue using the data you have loaded.
crumbs: Your First Data, Then the Concepts Behind It

title: 5  Fundamental Concepts
text:
5.1 Variable assignment
You already have static_file loaded from the last chapter. Everything in this chapter names a piece of what you’re already looking at. If you’ve shut down your R session, you will need to re-run the code to read in the data.
When you read the contents of the csv file, you assigned it to a variable called static_file (you can call it anything you like), so the code static_file <- read_csv(...) is an assignment. The arrow <- takes whatever’s on the right and stores it under the name on the left. Without the arrow, R would still read the contents of the file, but the data wouldn’t be saved to view or work with.
Anything can be assigned a name this way: a data frame, a single number, a short set of values. Once assigned, that name persists in your session (visible in the Variables pane from Meet Positron) until you clear it or close R.
Here’s an example. The code below removes all the duplicate values in the Subjects column and saves the unique subject names to a variable called test_subjects
library(tidyverse)

static_file <- read_csv("data/static_file_sample.csv")
test_subjects <- unique(static_file$Subject)

# Show the results
test_subjects

[1] "ELA"  "Math" "Sci"  NA
crumbs: Your First Data, Then the Concepts Behind It

title: 5  Fundamental Concepts
text:
5.2 Data frames
static_file is a data frame — a rectangular table of rows and columns, exactly like a tab in a spreadsheet or a table in a database. Each column holds one consistent type of data (text, whole numbers, decimals, dates, or TRUE/FALSE logical values).
You can see what type of object is assigned to a variable by using the class() function. Calling class() on static_file tells you it is a data frame.

class(static_file)

[1] "spec_tbl_df" "tbl_df"      "tbl"         "data.frame" 


It’s important to know that each column in the data frame can only include values that are the same data type. You’ve probable seen little green triangle in Excel cells warning you that numbers are stored as text. You might see this in something like a ScaleScore column. You might have cells with values of 240, 480, and 362 pts. Because the final value contains letters, it means everything in that column is stored as text and you won’t be able to use any of the numbers for mathematical operations. You would need clean the data and change it to a numeric column. When we used the glimpse() function, it showed the class of each column in the data frame.
crumbs: Your First Data, Then the Concepts Behind It

title: 5  Fundamental Concepts
text:
5.3 Vectors
A single column pulled out of a data frame is a vector — a sequence of values, all the same type. You can pull one out using $. For the example below, I’ll print only the first 10 “cells”.

static_file$ScaleScore



 [1] 425  13  10 421  20  NA 411 416 421  NA


A vector doesn’t have to come from a data frame, you can build one directly:

sample_grades <- c(3, 4, 5, 6)
sample_grades

[1] 3 4 5 6


c() (“combine”) is how you build a vector from individual values by hand. You’ll use it constantly, especially with filter() (as you’ll see in the next chapter).
crumbs: Your First Data, Then the Concepts Behind It

title: 5  Fundamental Concepts
text:
5.4 Vectorized behavior
This is one of the most important mental models to walk away with. In a spreadsheet, you write a formula once and drag it down an entire column. R does the equivalent automatically — operations apply to every “cell” of a column at once, with no dragging, no loop, no “fill down”.
For example, we can look at the ScaleScore column. For clarity, I’ll only show the values first 10 “cells”.

static_file$ScaleScore



 [1] 425  13  10 421  20  NA 411 416 421  NA


If I add five to the column, they syntax is just to say Column + 5. 5 will be added to every row in that column. Again, I’ll just show the values in the first 10 “cells”.

# Every scale score, increased by 5 points, all at once
static_file$ScaleScore + 5



 [1] 430  18  15 426  25  NA 416 421 426  NA


Here’s another example using a logical operation, asking whether each “cell” in the ScaleScore column is greater than 450. R will return a vector of TRUE or FALSE for each element.

# A TRUE/FALSE flag for every single row, simultaneously
static_file$ScaleScore > 450



 [1] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE    NA FALSE FALSE FALSE    NA


If you’ve ever worked with array formulas or named ranges in spreadsheets, this may feel familiar.
There’s no step where R goes row by row asking you what to do next — the whole column transforms in one motion. This concept is responsible for most of why R code tends to be short and easily readable: you’re describing the operation once, and it’s understood to apply everywhere. Once you get used to it, it starts to feel natural since it is written almost the same way you would say it out load: “ScaleScore plus 5” rather than a cell reference like C7 + 5 followed by C8 + 5. It also helps avoid mistakes like copy/paste errors, not dragging far enough, or accidentally deleting a formula from one cell.
crumbs: Your First Data, Then the Concepts Behind It

title: 5  Fundamental Concepts
text:
5.5 Lists
A vector requires every element to be the same type. A list is more flexible — it can hold a mix of types, including other data frames or vectors, bundled together. When I first started programming, I struggled with this concept and when it might be used. One of the most common uses is to store several data frames together. This would be similar to the concept of a “workbook” that holds several tabs. Each tab in the workbook can be completely different, similarly each element of a list can be different. That’s an oversimplified explanation, but bundling several data frames together is a common use for lists.
The code below creates a list with 3 elements: 2 data frames (student_list and school_list) and, to show how lists can hold different objects, a plot called chart. This would be analagous to a spreadsheet with two data tabs and one chart tab.

# Create the list
my_list <- list(
  students = distinct(static_file, SAISID, FirstName, LastName), 
  schools = distinct(static_file, DistrictCode, DistrictName, SchoolCode, SchoolName), 
  prof_growth_plot = prof_growth_viz
)

# glimpse the first item (student data)
glimpse(my_list$students)

Rows: 17,462
Columns: 3
$ SAISID    <dbl> 900044061, 900044064, 900044069, 900044071, 900044086, 90004…
$ FirstName <chr> "Ailen", "Waseem", "Maribel", "Sunshine", "Seauna", "Sameera…
$ LastName  <chr> "el-Ally", "Dorrell", "Draker", "al-Shaheen", "Hernandez", "…

# glimpse the second item (school data)
glimpse(my_list$schools)

$ DistrictName <chr> "District 6", "District 6", "District 7", "District 7", "…
$ SchoolCode   <dbl> 67, 95, 99, 100, 311, 493, 494, 564, 656, 657, 713, 732, …
$ SchoolName   <chr> "School 67", "School 95", "School 99", "School 100", "Sch…

# View the plot
my_list$prof_growth_plot





When you’re starting, you probably won’t use lists very often, but it’s good to know they exist, and you’ll see one of the most common uses (reading and combining multiple spreadsheets) later in this book (see Importing From Everywhere).
crumbs: Your First Data, Then the Concepts Behind It

title: 5  Fundamental Concepts
text:
5.6 The pipe
You’ve already seen $ used to pull a column out. The pipe, written |> , is how you chain a sequence of steps together. For me, it took some to wrap my head around the concept. I was used to spreadsheets functions which are evaluated “inside out”: the first function you read is the last one performed, and earlier functions are nested inside parentheses. The pipe syntax makes your code much more readable, it creates a list of sequential steps. Each step performs the function, and sends the result to the next line.
The code below uses the pipe operator to create a data set of charter schools in 4 steps:

Create a copy of static_file by assigning it to a variable called charter_schools
Filter the data to rows with a value of 1 in the Charter column
Select only the SchoolCode and SchoolName columns
Remove duplicates using the distinct function


charter_schools <- static_file |>
  filter(Charter == 1) |>
  select(SchoolCode, SchoolName) |> 
  distinct()

# glimpse the new data frame
glimpse(charter_schools)

Rows: 15
Columns: 2
$ SchoolCode <dbl> 99, 100, 311, 493, 494, 564, 656, 657, 713, 732, 783, 809, …
$ SchoolName <chr> "School 99", "School 100", "School 311", "School 493", "Sch…


Without the pipe, the same operation requires nesting function calls inside each other, which gets hard to read fast as steps accumulate. Below is the same code, written without the pipe (similar to a spreadsheet set of nested formulas)

charter_schools <- distinct(select(filter(static_file, Charter == 1)))

You’ll see |> in almost every code example for the rest of this book — it’s less a separate concept to learn than a connector that makes everything else more readable.
You may also encounter an older version of the pipe, %>%, in other people’s code or older tutorials — it comes from the magrittr package and behaves almost identically. |> is now built directly into base R and is the current recommended default; this book uses it consistently.
crumbs: Your First Data, Then the Concepts Behind It

title: 5  Fundamental Concepts
section: 5.7 NAs — R’s version of a blank cell
text:
5.7 NAs — R’s version of a blank cell
You likely noticed NA values scattered throughout the examples above and in static_file when you ran glimpse() — particularly in columns like Performance, ScaleScore, or Passing, for students who weren’t tested. NA is R’s explicit way of representing missing data — the equivalent of a blank cell in Excel, except R refuses to silently ignore it the way a spreadsheet formula sometimes will.
This matters in practice because NA is contagious in calculations unless you account for it. Essentially, R will not assume the meaning of missing data on its own. You need to explicitly tell it how to handle any missing data in calculations. Take a look at the examples below.

#returns NA, because there is at least one missing value
mean(static_file$ScaleScore)          

[1] NA

# tell R explicitly to ignore NAs first
mean(static_file$ScaleScore, na.rm = TRUE)   

[1] 1489.616


na.rm = TRUE (“NA, remove”) is something you’ll type often. R’s refusal to quietly average over missing values without your explicit permission is a feature, not a bug — it forces you to make a conscious decision about how to handle missing data, and helps avoid accidents, rather than getting a number that silently includes (or excludes) it without your knowledge.
To check how many missing values you have in a column, you sum up any “cell” that is NA:

sum(is.na(static_file$ScaleScore))

[1] 12998
crumbs: Your First Data, Then the Concepts Behind It

title: 5  Fundamental Concepts
text:
5.8 Basic operators
These operators may look familiar, especially if you have used a SQL WHERE clause. The specific R syntax is different in some cases.
The most important syntax to notice is the equals operator: ==. I think of it this way: a single equal sign, = is like a statement. It’s used for assignment and says one value is equal to another. For example, the code below creates a variable called my_number and says it is equal to 1.

my_number = 1

# Print the value of the variable, number
print(my_number)

[1] 1


The double equal sign, == is more like a question, asking whether the two items are equal. The result of == will answer your question with a TRUE or FALSE. The code below asks whether my_number is equal to certain values:

# Is the number 3?
my_number == 3

[1] FALSE

# Is the number 1?
my_number == 1

[1] TRUE


Because the == operator returns a TRUE or FALSE value, it can be used in filtering operations, e.g., filter to include only results where the answer to the question is TRUE.
The operators below also create TRUE or FALSE tests. You’ll use these constantly inside filter(), which is covered in the chapter Common Functions.








Operator
Meaning
Example




==
equals (note: two equals signs — one is reserved for assignment in some contexts)
Charter == 1


!=
not equal to
County != "Maricopa"


>, <, >=, <=
greater/less than (or equal)
ScaleScore >= 450


&
and
Charter == 1 & County == "Pima"


|
or
Subject == "ELA" | Subject == "Math"


%in%
value is one of several
County %in% c("Maricopa", "Pima")


!
not / negation
!is.na(ScaleScore)
crumbs: Your First Data, Then the Concepts Behind It

title: 6  Quarto Essentials
text:
6.1 What a Quarto document actually is
Everything you’ve run so far has lived in a plain script. This chapter introduces the document format this entire book is written in, and that you’ll use for most real deliverables going forward.
A .qmd file mixes two things in one document: ordinary prose, written in plain Markdown, and executable code, sitting in marked-off sections called chunks. When you render the document, Quarto runs every code chunk, captures its output (tables, plots, printed values), and weaves that output directly into the surrounding prose to produce a finished document — HTML, Word, PDF, or slides.
If you’ve ever built an Excel workbook with cells, a chart, and a text box explaining what the chart means, sitting next to each other — a Quarto document does the same job, except the “chart” and the “explanation” are generated and rendered together from one source, every single time you run it, with no risk of the picture and the explanation drifting out of sync after someone edits one but not the other.
crumbs: Your First Data, Then the Concepts Behind It

title: 6  Quarto Essentials
text:
6.2 Creating a code chunk
In a .qmd file, a chunk is a fenced-off block, opened and closed with three backticks, with the language specified in curly braces:

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
static_file |> filter(Charter == 1) |> nrow()
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 17935
```


:::
:::

Inside Positron, you can insert one quickly with the keyboard shortcut Ctrl + Alt + I (Windows) or Cmd + Option + I (Mac), or by typing the fence characters directly.
To run just that one chunk, click the small green play arrow that appears in the chunk’s top-right corner in the editor, or place your cursor inside it and use the same Ctrl/Cmd + Enter shortcut from Meet Positron.
crumbs: Your First Data, Then the Concepts Behind It

title: 6  Quarto Essentials
text:
6.3 Chunk options
You can control how a chunk behaves and what shows up in your final document using #| options at the top of the chunk:


[1] 17935


A few you’ll use often:

echo: false — run the code, but don’t show the code itself in the final document (only its output)
eval: false — show the code, but don’t actually run it (useful for illustrating syntax without needing live data)
message: false / warning: false — suppress informational messages and warnings from cluttering your rendered output
crumbs: Your First Data, Then the Concepts Behind It

title: 6  Quarto Essentials
text:
6.4 The YAML header
Every Quarto document opens with a fenced block of settings called the YAML header, between two lines of three dashes:
---
title: "School Performance Summary"
format: html
---
This is where you set document-wide options — title, author, output format, and more. A couple of settings worth knowing now:
---
title: "School Performance Summary"
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
---
embed-resources: true bundles all images, styles, and supporting files directly into a single output .html file — important if you intend to share that file with someone else and don’t want broken images because a folder got separated from it. This is particularly relevant for slideshow output (revealjs), where you’ll want one self-contained file to hand off or present from.
For Word documents specifically, you can point Quarto at an existing .docx as a formatting template using reference-doc, so your output inherits an organization’s existing letterhead, fonts, and styles rather than Quarto’s defaults:
format:
  docx:
    reference-doc: ade-template.docx
crumbs: Your First Data, Then the Concepts Behind It

title: 6  Quarto Essentials
text:
6.5 Inline code
You can drop a single computed value directly into a sentence of prose, rather than only in a chunk’s table or plot output:

# This dataset includes `r length(unique(static_file$SchoolName))` schools.

When rendered, that backtick-r-expression is replaced with the actual computed number — and critically, it updates automatically every time you re-render against new data. This is a small thing with a large payoff for accountability reporting specifically: a sentence like “32 schools met the threshold this year” never goes stale, because it’s not typed text, it’s a live calculation.
crumbs: Your First Data, Then the Concepts Behind It

title: 6  Quarto Essentials
text:
6.6 Previewing and rendering
Two related but distinct actions:

Preview — click the Preview button at the top of the editor (or Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + K) to render the document and see the result, typically opening in the Viewer pane or your browser. This is what you’ll do constantly while building a document.
Render — produces the actual output file (.html, .docx, etc.) saved to disk, ready to share. Preview generally does this for you automatically behind the scenes.

You now have everything mechanically necessary to follow along in your own .qmd file for the rest of this book. The next several chapters return to the data itself — starting with the small set of functions that will carry the large majority of your day-to-day work.
crumbs: Your First Data, Then the Concepts Behind It

title: 7  Common Functions
text:
7.1 Cleaning messy column names first
This chapter covers the small set of functions that will carry the majority of your day-to-day work. Each one maps to something you already do in Excel, SAS, or Access — the goal here is naming the R equivalent and giving you a working recipe, not re-teaching the underlying logic you already have.
All examples continue using static_file, loaded back in Your First Import.
Real files rarely arrive with clean, consistent column names. Before doing anything else, it’s worth knowing about janitor::clean_names() — genuinely one of the highest-value, lowest-effort functions in this entire book:
library(janitor)

static_file_new_names <- static_file |> clean_names()

names(static_file_new_names)[1:10]

 [1] "static_file_data_id" "school_code"         "school_name"        
 [4] "school_ctds"         "district_code"       "district_name"      
 [7] "district_ctds"       "charter"             "county"             
[10] "aoi"
This converts inconsistent capitalization, spaces, and punctuation in column names into a single consistent format (typically snake_case) automatically. Our Static File sample already has clean names, so you won’t see dramatic changes here — but the moment you load a real Excel export with column headers like "School Name " (trailing space included) or "% Proficient", this single line will save you real frustration. Use it by habit on every new file you import.

static_file |>

    Performance == 3 ~ "Proficient",

7.9 str_detect() — pattern matching inside text
Part of the stringr package (included in the tidyverse), useful for searching within a text column — similar in spirit to Excel’s SEARCH() or a SQL LIKE clause:
