title: 7  Common Functions
text:
7.11 Putting several together
This is where the pipe (|>) starts to earn its keep — chaining several of the functions above into a single readable sequence:

static_file |>
  filter(Subject == "ELA", !is.na(Performance)) |>
  select(SchoolName, DistrictName, Performance) |>
  mutate(performance_label = case_when(
    Performance == 1 ~ "Minimally Proficient",
    Performance == 2 ~ "Partially Proficient",
    Performance == 3 ~ "Proficient",
    Performance == 4 ~ "Highly Proficient"
  )) |>
  arrange(DistrictName)

# A tibble: 7,254 × 4
   SchoolName DistrictName Performance performance_label   
   <chr>      <chr>              <dbl> <chr>               
 1 School 311 District 104           1 Minimally Proficient
 2 School 311 District 104           1 Minimally Proficient
 3 School 311 District 104           1 Minimally Proficient
 4 School 311 District 104           1 Minimally Proficient
 5 School 311 District 104           3 Proficient          
 6 School 311 District 104           1 Minimally Proficient
 7 School 311 District 104           2 Partially Proficient
 8 School 311 District 104           2 Partially Proficient
 9 School 311 District 104           1 Minimally Proficient
10 School 311 District 104           2 Partially Proficient
# ℹ 7,244 more rows


Read top to bottom as one sentence: take the Static File, keep only assessed ELA records, keep just these columns, add a readable label, then sort by district. Each line is a single, understandable step — and the whole sequence reads roughly the way you’d describe the task out loud to a colleague.
The next chapter builds directly on this same pattern to tackle the task you’ll do more than almost any other in accountability work: summarizing data by group.

title: 8  Group, Summarize, and the Percentage-by-Group Pattern
text:
8.1 group_by() and summarize()
If pivot tables are the single Excel skill you use most in accountability work, this chapter is its direct R equivalent — and the pattern you’ll reach for constantly.
static_file |>
  group_by(SchoolName) |>
  summarize(
    n_students    = n(),
    avg_scale_score = mean(ScaleScore, na.rm = TRUE),
    pct_passing   = mean(Passing, na.rm = TRUE)
  )

# A tibble: 30 × 4
   SchoolName  n_students avg_scale_score pct_passing
   <chr>            <int>           <dbl>       <dbl>
 1 School 100         355           2782.       0.422
 2 School 1047        545            453.       0.06 
 3 School 1125       1438            420.       0.108
 4 School 1128       1404            449.       0.210
 5 School 1181        700           2726.       0.547
 6 School 1304        671           2765.       0.628
 7 School 1306       1361           1648.       0.371
 8 School 1308       1387            694.       0.485
 9 School 1312       1229            948.       0.404
10 School 1313        653           2719.       0.564
# ℹ 20 more rows
Map this directly onto a pivot table: group_by() defines your “rows” (here, one row per school), and each line inside summarize() is a “values” field with whatever aggregation you want — n() for a count, mean() for an average, sum(), median(), sd() for standard deviation, and so on.
One detail worth noticing: mean(Passing, na.rm = TRUE) is doing double duty here. Since Passing is coded as 0/1, averaging it directly produces the proportion passing — a small trick worth remembering, since it means you often don’t need a separate percentage calculation for any binary (0/1) indicator column, which describes a lot of the Static File’s columns (Charter, EL, SPED, ChronicAbsent, and others).
crumbs: 8  Group, Summarize, and the Percentage-by-Group Pattern

title: 8  Group, Summarize, and the Percentage-by-Group Pattern
section: 8.2 Grouping by more than one field
text:
8.2 Grouping by more than one field

static_file |>
  group_by(DistrictName, Subject) |>
  summarize(avg_scale_score = mean(ScaleScore, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

# A tibble: 79 × 3
   DistrictName Subject avg_scale_score
   <chr>        <chr>             <dbl>
 1 District 104 ELA                980.
 2 District 104 Math              1390.
 3 District 104 Sci               1315.
 4 District 104 <NA>               NaN 
 5 District 182 ELA               2022.
 6 District 182 Math              2857.
 7 District 182 Sci               1310.
 8 District 182 <NA>               NaN 
 9 District 183 ELA               1730.
10 District 183 Math              2440.
# ℹ 69 more rows


.groups = "drop" is worth including out of habit — without it, summarize() leaves the result still grouped by all but the last grouping variable, which can cause confusing behavior in whatever you do next. Explicitly dropping the grouping keeps your next step working on a clean, ungrouped result.
crumbs: 8  Group, Summarize, and the Percentage-by-Group Pattern

title: 8  Group, Summarize, and the Percentage-by-Group Pattern
section: 8.3 The mutate → group_by → summarize → ungroup pattern
text:
8.3 The mutate → group_by → summarize → ungroup pattern
This is the single most useful combination move in this entire book, and it comes up constantly in accountability work: calculating what percentage of the total each group represents.
Walking through it step by step, using EL status as the example — what percentage of all assessed students in each school are EL students?

static_file |>
  filter(!is.na(Performance)) |>                         # only assessed students
  mutate(is_one = 1) |>                                    # step 1: a column of 1s, one per record
  group_by(SchoolName) |>                                  # step 2: set up groups
  summarize(
    school_total = sum(is_one),                            # step 3: total records per school
    el_total     = sum(EL)                                 # and EL records per school, in the same step
  ) |>
  ungroup() |>                                              # step 4: release the grouping
  mutate(pct_el = el_total / school_total)                  # step 5: divide to get a percentage

# A tibble: 30 × 4
   SchoolName  school_total el_total  pct_el
   <chr>              <dbl>    <dbl>   <dbl>
 1 School 100           351        0 0      
 2 School 1047           40        6 0.15   
 3 School 1125          538       79 0.147  
 4 School 1128          583       19 0.0326 
 5 School 1181          686       12 0.0175 
 6 School 1304          658        5 0.00760
 7 School 1306          888       12 0.0135 
 8 School 1308          867        8 0.00923
 9 School 1312          600        3 0.005  
10 School 1313          629        6 0.00954
# ℹ 20 more rows


Why each step earns its place:

mutate(is_one = 1) gives you something reliable to sum() as a row counter. In this particular case n() inside summarize() would do the same job more directly — but this pattern matters once you want two different kinds of totals out of the same grouped calculation (as shown above, getting both school_total and el_total in one pass), which n() alone can’t do.
group_by(SchoolName) sets up the grouping — same as before.
summarize() collapses each group down to one row, computing both totals you need simultaneously.
ungroup() is the step it’s easy to forget. After a summarize(), your result frame is still technically grouped (try running the chunk above without it, then run mutate() afterward separately — you’ll get the right answer here, but in more complex cases a lingering group can silently produce a percentage calculated within a group you no longer intend). Ungrouping first is the safe habit.
mutate(pct_el = el_total / school_total) is the actual division — now safely happening across the whole ungrouped result, not row-by-row-within-group.

Once you’re comfortable with this five-step shape, you’ll find yourself reusing it for almost any “percent of total within a group” question — percent proficient, percent chronically absent, percent SPED, percent passing by subgroup. The columns change; the pattern doesn’t.
crumbs: 8  Group, Summarize, and the Percentage-by-Group Pattern

title: 8  Group, Summarize, and the Percentage-by-Group Pattern
section: 8.4 A shortcut for the simplest version
text:
8.4 A shortcut for the simplest version
If you only need one straightforward percentage and don’t need multiple custom totals in the same step, you can skip the mutate(is_one = 1) setup and lean on n() and mean() directly, as shown at the top of this chapter — that’s usually the right call for a quick one-off. Reach for the fuller five-step pattern above when you need more than one total out of the same grouped pass, or when the percentage isn’t a simple 0/1 average.
crumbs: 8  Group, Summarize, and the Percentage-by-Group Pattern

title: 9  Reshaping
text:
9.1 The problem reshaping solves
This is the one core skill in this book with no clean single-click Excel equivalent — which is exactly why it’s worth learning. It solves problems that are genuinely painful to do by hand, and it shows up constantly once you start preparing data for charts or multi-subject comparisons.
The Static File has one row per student per subject-and-record-type combination, with a single Subject column (ELA, Math, Sci) and a single Performance column holding that subject’s result. If you wanted to compare a student’s ELA performance side-by-side with their Math performance — one row per student, one column per subject — the data isn’t currently shaped that way. Reshaping is how you get there.

title: 9  Reshaping
text:
9.2 Wide to long: pivot_longer()
“Long” format is what most of dplyr and ggplot2 expect — generally, more rows, fewer columns, with one column identifying what’s being measured and another holding the value. Our Static File is already mostly long-shaped with respect to Subject/Performance — but to demonstrate the mechanics clearly, imagine you’d instead been handed a wider file with separate columns per subject:

# Imagine a wide-format school summary like this:
# SchoolName | ela_avg | math_avg | sci_avg

wide_example <- static_file |>
  group_by(SchoolName) |>
  summarize(
    ela_avg  = mean(ScaleScore[Subject == "ELA"], na.rm = TRUE),
    math_avg = mean(ScaleScore[Subject == "Math"], na.rm = TRUE),
    sci_avg  = mean(ScaleScore[Subject == "Sci"], na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

wide_example

# A tibble: 30 × 4
   SchoolName  ela_avg math_avg sci_avg
   <chr>         <dbl>    <dbl>   <dbl>
 1 School 100    2537.    3591.   1328.
 2 School 1047    127.     126.   1293.
 3 School 1125    224.     217.   1306.
 4 School 1128    228.     222.   1310.
 5 School 1181   2561.    3652.   1346.
 6 School 1304   2566.    3653.   1356.
 7 School 1306   1456.    2016.   1336.
 8 School 1308    386.     505.   1339.
 9 School 1312    742.     970.   1334.
10 School 1313   2554.    3642.   1348.
# ℹ 20 more rows


Now reshape that wide summary into long format — one row per school per subject, rather than one row per school with three separate subject columns:

long_example <- wide_example |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(ela_avg, math_avg, sci_avg),
    names_to = "subject",
    values_to = "avg_scale_score"
  )

long_example

# A tibble: 90 × 3
   SchoolName  subject  avg_scale_score
   <chr>       <chr>              <dbl>
 1 School 100  ela_avg            2537.
 2 School 100  math_avg           3591.
 3 School 100  sci_avg            1328.
 4 School 1047 ela_avg             127.
 5 School 1047 math_avg            126.
 6 School 1047 sci_avg            1293.
 7 School 1125 ela_avg             224.
 8 School 1125 math_avg            217.
 9 School 1125 sci_avg            1306.
10 School 1128 ela_avg             228.
# ℹ 80 more rows


This is the shape you’d want for a ggplot2 chart comparing average scale scores by subject and school (covered in Visualization) — long format is what lets you map a single “subject” column to color or facet, rather than writing three separate geom_*() calls, one per wide column.

title: 9  Reshaping
section: 9.3 Long to wide: pivot_wider() — the reverse
text:
9.3 Long to wide: pivot_wider() — the reverse
Sometimes you want the opposite — collapsing long data back into a wide, side-by-side comparison table, which is often exactly what you want for a final report table a human will read:

long_example |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = subject, values_from = avg_scale_score)

# A tibble: 30 × 4
   SchoolName  ela_avg math_avg sci_avg
   <chr>         <dbl>    <dbl>   <dbl>
 1 School 100    2537.    3591.   1328.
 2 School 1047    127.     126.   1293.
 3 School 1125    224.     217.   1306.
 4 School 1128    228.     222.   1310.
 5 School 1181   2561.    3652.   1346.
 6 School 1304   2566.    3653.   1356.
 7 School 1306   1456.    2016.   1336.
 8 School 1308    386.     505.   1339.
 9 School 1312    742.     970.   1334.
10 School 1313   2554.    3642.   1348.
# ℹ 20 more rows


This returns you to the original wide shape — useful as a final reporting step even if your analysis happened in long format along the way.

title: 9  Reshaping
section: 9.4 A more direct example using the Static File as-is
text:
9.4 A more direct example using the Static File as-is
Since Subject and Performance are already separate columns in long form, here’s a reshape you could realistically run directly: turning a count of students by performance level into a side-by-side comparison table across subjects.

static_file |>
  filter(!is.na(Performance)) |>
  count(Subject, Performance) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = Subject, values_from = n, values_fill = 0)

# A tibble: 4 × 4
  Performance   ELA  Math   Sci
        <dbl> <int> <int> <int>
1           1  2635  3512  1576
2           2  1522  1691  2087
3           3  2236  1276   940
4           4   861   956   213


values_fill = 0 is worth knowing about — if some Subject/Performance combination has zero students, pivot_wider() would otherwise leave that cell as NA. Setting values_fill = 0 tells it to use an actual zero instead, which is almost always what you want for a count table.
The general rule of thumb: reach for pivot_longer() when you’re heading toward a chart or further group_by() analysis, and pivot_wider() when you’re heading toward a final, human-readable comparison table.

title: 10  Importing From Everywhere
text:
10.1 From a single Excel file
You’ve already imported one CSV. This chapter covers the rest of the places accountability data actually lives — Excel workbooks with multiple tabs, Google Sheets, and the brute-force-to-elegant progression that’s worth understanding in that order.
library(readxl)

df <- read_excel("AF_Results_2026.xlsx")                    # first sheet by default
df <- read_excel("AF_Results_2026.xlsx", sheet = "Schools")  # by sheet name
df <- read_excel("AF_Results_2026.xlsx", sheet = 2)          # by sheet position
df <- read_excel("AF_Results_2026.xlsx", range = "A1:H500")  # specific range, like a named range

excel_sheets("AF_Results_2026.xlsx")  # list every sheet name without opening the file
Tip: read_excel() guesses each column’s type by sampling rows. A numeric ID column with leading zeros, or one with occasional blanks, sometimes gets misread as text. Force types explicitly when that happens:
df <- read_excel("AF_Results_2026.xlsx", col_types = c("text", "numeric", "date"))

title: 10  Importing From Everywhere
section: 10.2 Combining multiple tabs — the brute-force way first
text:
10.2 Combining multiple tabs — the brute-force way first
Accountability files very often arrive as one workbook with a separate tab per year or per assessment window — exactly the situation bind_rows() solves. It’s worth learning the obvious, slightly repetitive way first, so you understand exactly what’s happening before reaching for the shortcut:

year_2023 <- read_excel("AF_Results_Multi_Year.xlsx", sheet = "2023")
year_2024 <- read_excel("AF_Results_Multi_Year.xlsx", sheet = "2024")
year_2025 <- read_excel("AF_Results_Multi_Year.xlsx", sheet = "2025")

combined <- bind_rows(year_2023, year_2024, year_2025)

bind_rows() stacks data frames on top of each other — same idea as copy-pasting one tab’s data underneath another’s, except it also handles columns that don’t perfectly line up between sheets (filling in NA where a column is missing from one of them) and keeps a record of exactly what happened, rather than a manual paste you’d have to redo by hand next year.
This approach works completely fine for three tabs. It gets tedious and error-prone at ten.

title: 10  Importing From Everywhere
section: 10.3 The same task, the elegant way
text:
10.3 The same task, the elegant way
Once you have more tabs than you want to type out by hand, combine reading and binding into one motion using lapply() and map_dfr():

sheet_names <- excel_sheets("AF_Results_Multi_Year.xlsx")

list_of_dfs <- lapply(sheet_names, function(s) {
  read_excel("AF_Results_Multi_Year.xlsx", sheet = s)
})

combined <- map_dfr(list_of_dfs, identity)

Walking through what’s actually happening:

excel_sheets() grabs every sheet name in the file automatically — you’re no longer typing tab names by hand, so this scales to any number of tabs without more code.
lapply() (“list apply”) runs the same function — here, read_excel() for a given sheet — once per item in sheet_names, and collects every result into a single list. This is the list data structure from Fundamental Concepts doing real work: a list of several data frames, one per sheet.
map_dfr() (“map, data-frame row-bind”) takes that list and stacks every data frame inside it into one combined data frame — functionally the same outcome as the manual bind_rows() above, but it works identically whether your list has 3 sheets or 30.

A more compact, commonly-seen version of the same idea, often written directly with map_dfr() alone:

combined <- sheet_names |>
  map_dfr(~ read_excel("AF_Results_Multi_Year.xlsx", sheet = .x))

The ~ and .x here are purrr package shorthand for “for each sheet name, run read_excel() with this sheet” — don’t worry about memorizing the exact syntax yet; recognizing the pattern (read every sheet, bind them all) is more important than the shorthand at this stage.

title: 10  Importing From Everywhere
section: 10.4 From CSV or other flat files
text:
10.4 From CSV or other flat files

library(readr)

df <- read_csv("enrollment.csv")          # comma-separated
df <- read_delim("data.txt", delim = "|") # pipe-delimited, tab, or any other separator

title: 10  Importing From Everywhere
text:
10.5 From Google Sheets

library(googlesheets4)

gs4_auth()  # one-time interactive browser login; caches credentials after that

df <- read_sheet("https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/your-sheet-id-here")
df <- read_sheet("your-sheet-id-here", sheet = "Raw Data")

A wrinkle worth knowing about in advance: gs4_auth() is designed for an interactive session — it expects to be able to pop open a browser window and wait for you to click through a login. That breaks the moment you try to render a Quarto document non-interactively (for example, an automated nightly report), since there’s no human present to click anything.
The fix is to authorize non-interactively, using a cached token instead of a live browser prompt:

options(gargle_oauth_cache = ".secrets")   # set a fixed location for the cached token, once
gs4_auth()                                  # run this once, interactively, to create the cached token

# In your rendered/automated script, going forward:
gs4_auth(cache = ".secrets", email = "your_service_account_or_email@example.gov")

In practice: authenticate once interactively to create the cached token, then point any automated or rendered script at that same cache going forward rather than calling the interactive flow again. For genuinely unattended automation (a scheduled job with no human involved at all), a Google service account is the more robust long-term answer, authenticated via gs4_auth(path = "service-account-key.json") — worth investigating once your Google Sheets workflow moves from occasional manual renders to a real recurring automation.
The next chapter covers the remaining major source you’ll encounter in accountability work — databases — along with the credential-security practices that matter once real login information is involved.

title: 11  Database Connections
text:
11.1 A basic connection
If you already write SQL in Access, SSMS, or anywhere else, this chapter is the smallest jump in the entire book — your query logic doesn’t change at all. What changes is how you establish the connection from R, and — just as important — how you do that safely.
library(DBI)
library(odbc)

con <- dbConnect(odbc::odbc(), "Your_DSN_Name")   # DSN set up via ODBC Data Source Administrator

df <- dbGetQuery(con, "SELECT * FROM dbo.enrollment WHERE school_year = 2026")

dbDisconnect(con)  # close the connection when you're done
If you don’t already have a DSN (Data Source Name) configured, you can instead supply full connection details directly:
con <- dbConnect(odbc::odbc(),
                  Driver   = "SQL Server",
                  Server   = "your-server.domain.gov",
                  Database = "Accountability",
                  UID      = "your_username",
                  PWD      = "your_password")

title: 11  Database Connections
section: 11.2 Stop — that last example has a real problem
text:
11.2 Stop — that last example has a real problem
Look at that connection again. The password is sitting in plain text, directly in a script. If that script ever gets committed to version control, emailed to a colleague, or simply left open on a shared screen, that credential is exposed. Never type database credentials directly into a script — this is a real, common mistake, not a theoretical one, and it’s worth fixing before it becomes a habit.
A few better options, roughly in order of preference:

11.2.1 Option 1: Windows / AD credentials (ideal where available)
If your organization’s database supports trusted authentication tied to your existing Windows login, you can skip passwords entirely:

con <- dbConnect(odbc::odbc(),
                  Driver   = "SQL Server",
                  Server   = "your-server.domain.gov",
                  Database = "Accountability",
                  Trusted_Connection = "Yes")

No username or password anywhere in the script — your existing AD login does the work. This is the cleanest option when your environment supports it.


11.2.2 Option 2: The keyring package (strong option on Mac, and works on Windows too)
The keyring package stores credentials securely in your operating system’s native credential manager — Mac’s Keychain, or Windows Credential Manager — rather than in any file you write:

library(keyring)

# One-time setup: store the credential securely (prompts you interactively, doesn't appear in your script)
key_set("ade_database_password", username = "your_username")

# In your actual script, going forward — retrieves the password without ever displaying or storing it in code
con <- dbConnect(odbc::odbc(),
                  Driver   = "SQL Server",
                  Server   = "your-server.domain.gov",
                  Database = "Accountability",
                  UID      = "your_username",
                  PWD      = key_get("ade_database_password", username = "your_username"))

On Mac, keyring integrates with the native Keychain and the Ticket Viewer app directly, which makes setup straightforward. On Windows, keyring also works, backed by Windows Credential Manager — the package is cross-platform, so the same key_set() / key_get() pattern applies on both systems without you needing to learn a separate workflow per OS.


11.2.3 Option 3: A config file outside your project, never committed to version control
At minimum, if neither of the above is available in your environment, reference a separate config file stored somewhere secure — outside your project folder, and explicitly excluded from version control (a .gitignore entry) so it never accidentally gets shared:

# config.yml, sitting in a secure location OUTSIDE your project folder — never inside it
# database:
#   server: your-server.domain.gov
#   uid: your_username
#   pwd: your_password

library(config)
db_config <- config::get(file = "/secure/location/config.yml", config = "database")

con <- dbConnect(odbc::odbc(),
                  Driver   = "SQL Server",
                  Server   = db_config$server,
                  UID      = db_config$uid,
                  PWD      = db_config$pwd)

This is meaningfully better than hardcoding, but still weaker than Options 1 or 2 — a config file is still a file that could be misplaced or copied. Treat it as a fallback, not a first choice.

title: 11  Database Connections
section: 11.3 DuckDB and Parquet — a lighter-weight alternative worth knowing about
text:
11.3 DuckDB and Parquet — a lighter-weight alternative worth knowing about
Not every situation calls for a full database connection. For working with large local files efficiently — without needing a server, a DSN, or any credentials at all — DuckDB and the Parquet file format are increasingly common in the modern R (and Python) data ecosystem, and are part of the broader Posit/polyglot convergence mentioned back in Why R First.

library(duckdb)
library(DBI)

con <- dbConnect(duckdb())

# Query a Parquet file directly, without even loading it fully into memory first
result <- dbGetQuery(con, "SELECT * FROM 'static_file_sample.parquet' WHERE Subject = 'ELA'")

dbDisconnect(con)

If you already know SQL, this can make the transition to R noticeably easier — you keep writing the query logic you already know, while gaining R’s data-wrangling and visualization tools around it. Parquet files are also typically far smaller and faster to read than an equivalent CSV (worth knowing if you ever find yourself working with a Static File at full statewide scale, rather than the sample used in this book), and CSV remains a perfectly fine fallback wherever Parquet isn’t already in use.
The next chapter moves from getting data in to one of the most valuable things you can do once it’s there — visualization.

title: 12  Visualization
text:
12.1 The grammar of graphics, in plain terms
Excel’s Chart Wizard asks you one question up front: what type of chart do you want? ggplot2 — the charting package you’ll use throughout this chapter — asks a different question first: what does each variable in your data mean in the picture you’re about to build? This way of thinking is called the grammar of graphics, and once it clicks, it tends to make charting in R feel less like memorizing chart types and more like describing what you already understand about your data.
Two pieces of vocabulary worth having before the first example:

title: 12  Visualization
text:
Aesthetics (aes()) — which columns map to which visual properties. Does ScaleScore control position on the x-axis? Does Subject control color? You’re deciding what each variable’s job is in the picture, before deciding how anything gets drawn.
Layers — think of a chart as a stack of transparent sheets, each one adding something: one layer draws points, another adds a trend line, another adds labels. You build a chart by stacking layers with +, and you can add, remove, or reorder them independently.

title: 12  Visualization
text:
12.2 The basic pattern

library(ggplot2)

ggplot(data = static_file, aes(x = ScaleScore, y = SGP_CCR)) +
  geom_point()

Read this as a sentence: “Using static_file, map ScaleScore to the x-axis and SGP_CCR to the y-axis, then draw points.” aes() set up the meaning; geom_point() is the layer that actually draws something using that meaning.

title: 12  Visualization
section: 12.3 Common chart types — same data, different geom
text:
12.3 Common chart types — same data, different geom
The aesthetics often barely change between chart types — usually, only the geom_*() layer does:

static_file |>
  filter(!is.na(Performance)) |>
  count(Subject, Performance) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = factor(Performance), y = n, fill = Subject)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge")             # bar chart

ggplot(static_file, aes(x = ScaleScore)) +
  geom_histogram()                          # histogram

ggplot(static_file, aes(x = factor(StudentGrade), y = ScaleScore)) +
  geom_boxplot()                            # boxplot, score distribution by grade

title: 12  Visualization
section: 12.4 Adding color, labels, and a cleaner look
text:
12.4 Adding color, labels, and a cleaner look

static_file |>
  filter(!is.na(Performance), Subject == "ELA") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = ScaleScore, fill = factor(Performance))) +
  geom_histogram(alpha = 0.8) +
  labs(
    title = "ELA Scale Score Distribution by Performance Level",
    x = "Scale Score",
    y = "Number of Students",
    fill = "Performance Level"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

labs() is where your titles and axis labels live; theme_minimal() is one of several built-in themes that strip away Excel’s default gray gridline clutter. theme_bw(), theme_classic(), and others are worth trying — swapping a theme is a one-word change, not a re-build.

title: 12  Visualization
section: 12.5 Faceting — one chart per group, automatically
text:
12.5 Faceting — one chart per group, automatically

static_file |>
  filter(!is.na(ScaleScore)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = ScaleScore)) +
  geom_histogram() +
  facet_wrap(~ Subject)

This single line replaces manually building and formatting a separate chart per subject — facet_wrap() does it in one pass, with consistent formatting guaranteed across every panel.

title: 12  Visualization
text:
12.6 Making it interactive: plotly::ggplotly()
Everything above produces a static image. Wrapping a finished ggplot2 chart in ggplotly() turns it interactive — hover tooltips, zoom, and pan — with no rebuilding required:

library(plotly)

p <- static_file |>
  filter(!is.na(Performance)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = factor(Performance), fill = Subject)) +
  geom_bar(position = "dodge") +
  labs(title = "Student Counts by Performance Level and Subject")

ggplotly(p)

This is particularly effective inside a Quarto HTML document or presentation (see Project Walkthrough 2) — a static chart in a PDF becomes something a viewer can actually explore.

title: 12  Visualization
section: 12.7 Tables: gt for polished static tables

title: 12  Visualization
section: 12.8 Tables: DT for interactive, explorable tables
text:
12.8 Tables: DT for interactive, explorable tables
When the audience needs to search, sort, filter, or export the data themselves — rather than just read a fixed top-10 list — the DT package builds an interactive table widget, well suited to an HTML Quarto document:

library(DT)

static_file |>
  select(SchoolName, DistrictName, Subject, Performance, ScaleScore) |>
  head(250) |> 
  datatable(
    filter = "top",                 # column-level filter boxes
    extensions = "Buttons",
    options = list(
      scrollY = "400px",            # scroll instead of paginating
      paging = FALSE,
      dom = "Bfrtip",
      buttons = c("csv")            # a CSV download button, built in
    )
  )

A reader can filter to one school, sort by scale score, or export exactly the slice they care about — all without you building separate views for them. This is the table format you’ll use in Project Walkthrough 2.

title: 12  Visualization
section: 12.9 Inline values, tying it back to Quarto
text:
12.9 Inline values, tying it back to Quarto
As covered in Quarto Essentials, any of the values you calculate in this chapter can be dropped directly into a sentence of prose using inline code:

# The average ELA scale score across all schools in this sample is `r round(mean(static_file$ScaleScore[static_file$Subject == "ELA"], na.rm = TRUE), 1)`.

Combined with a gt table and a ggplotly() chart, this is the core toolkit behind both project walkthroughs that close out this book — starting with the next chapter.

title: 13  Project Walkthrough 1: Automating a Multi-Tab Import
text:
13.1 The goal, end to end
This is the first of two end-to-end projects that pull together everything in this book so far. The scenario: you receive an Excel workbook with one tab per year of assessment data, and you need a clean, tidy, combined dataset — plus a validation file confirming the import looks right — produced automatically every time, rather than rebuilt by hand each cycle.
This belongs in a plain .R script rather than a .qmd document, since the goal is unattended automation, not a narrated report (see the file-type distinction in Initial Setup).
crumbs: 13  Project Walkthrough 1: Automating a Multi-Tab Import

title: 13  Project Walkthrough 1: Automating a Multi-Tab Import
text:
Read every tab from a multi-year Excel workbook
Combine them into one tidy data frame
Reshape into long format for easier downstream analysis
Produce summary statistics as a validation check
Write both the tidy data and the validation summary to separate output folders
crumbs: 13  Project Walkthrough 1: Automating a Multi-Tab Import

title: 13  Project Walkthrough 1: Automating a Multi-Tab Import
text:
13.2 Step 1: Read every tab

library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)
library(janitor)

file_path <- "data/multi_year_assessment_results.xlsx"

sheet_names <- excel_sheets(file_path)

raw_list <- sheet_names |>
  map_dfr(~ read_excel(file_path, sheet = .x) |> mutate(source_sheet = .x))

Notice the addition of mutate(source_sheet = .x) inside the map step — tagging every row with which tab it came from before combining. This is cheap insurance: if anything looks wrong later, you can immediately trace a record back to its source tab rather than guessing.
crumbs: 13  Project Walkthrough 1: Automating a Multi-Tab Import

title: 13  Project Walkthrough 1: Automating a Multi-Tab Import
crumbs: 13  Project Walkthrough 1: Automating a Multi-Tab Import

title: 13  Project Walkthrough 1: Automating a Multi-Tab Import
text:
13.4 Step 3: Reshape to long format
Suppose each year’s tab had separate columns per subject (ela_score, math_score, sci_score) — a common real-world shape for a multi-year export. Reshape into one row per student per subject:

tidy_long <- tidy_data |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = ends_with("_score"),
    names_to = "subject",
    names_pattern = "(.*)_score",
    values_to = "scale_score"
  )

names_pattern here extracts just the subject portion of each column name (ela, math, sci) rather than keeping the full _score suffix — a small refinement on the basic pivot_longer() pattern from Reshaping.
crumbs: 13  Project Walkthrough 1: Automating a Multi-Tab Import

title: 13  Project Walkthrough 1: Automating a Multi-Tab Import
text:
13.5 Step 4: Build a validation summary
Before trusting any pipeline’s output, generate counts and summary statistics you can quickly eyeball against what you’d expect:

validation_summary <- tidy_long |>
  group_by(source_sheet, subject) |>
  summarize(
    n_records      = n(),
    n_missing      = sum(is.na(scale_score)),
    avg_scale_score = mean(scale_score, na.rm = TRUE),
    min_score      = min(scale_score, na.rm = TRUE),
    max_score      = max(scale_score, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

This is the kind of table you’d glance at every time the pipeline runs — a sudden jump in n_missing, or an avg_scale_score outside the range you expect, is often the first sign something upstream changed before you’d otherwise notice.
crumbs: 13  Project Walkthrough 1: Automating a Multi-Tab Import

title: 13  Project Walkthrough 1: Automating a Multi-Tab Import
section: 13.6 Step 5: Write outputs to separate subfolders
text:
13.6 Step 5: Write outputs to separate subfolders

dir.create("output/tidy", recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)
dir.create("output/validation", recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)

write_csv(tidy_long, "output/tidy/assessment_results_tidy.csv")
write_csv(validation_summary, "output/validation/validation_summary.csv")

dir.create(..., showWarnings = FALSE) quietly creates the folder if it doesn’t exist yet, and does nothing (without throwing an error) if it already does — useful for a script meant to run unattended and repeatedly.
crumbs: 13  Project Walkthrough 1: Automating a Multi-Tab Import

title: 13  Project Walkthrough 1: Automating a Multi-Tab Import
section: 13.7 Why this is worth the setup
text:
13.7 Why this is worth the setup
The entire sequence above — reading, cleaning, reshaping, validating, and writing — is now a single script. Next year, when a new tab gets added to the source workbook, nothing about this script needs to change. excel_sheets() picks up the new tab automatically, map_dfr() combines it in automatically, and the validation summary will include it without you touching a single line. This is the compounding-returns argument for scripted work in its purest form: the investment happened once, here, and every future run is free.
The next chapter builds the second kind of deliverable — not an automated pipeline, but an interactive Quarto presentation meant for a human audience to actually read and explore.
crumbs: 13  Project Walkthrough 1: Automating a Multi-Tab Import

title: 14  Project Walkthrough 2: A Quarto Interactive Presentation
text:
14.1 Setting up a new Quarto document
The first project produced a clean, validated dataset behind the scenes. This one builds something a human being will actually open and read: an interactive Quarto presentation summarizing results, suitable for sharing with a principal, a board, or your own team.
Start with a new .qmd file (see Quarto Essentials for the mechanics), with a YAML header set up for revealjs slides and self-contained sharing:
# ---
# title: "School Performance Summary"
# subtitle: "Static File Sample — Current Year"
# format:
#   revealjs:
#     embed-resources: true
#     controls: true
# execute:
#   echo: false
#   warning: false
#   message: false
# ---
embed-resources: true matters here specifically because this file is meant to be shared — a single self-contained .html that opens correctly on anyone’s machine, with no separate image folder that could go missing. echo: false at the document level means code won’t show by default in any chunk unless you explicitly override it per-chunk — appropriate for a presentation meant for an audience, as opposed to the code-forward reference chapters earlier in this book.
crumbs: 14  Project Walkthrough 2: A Quarto Interactive Presentation

title: 14  Project Walkthrough 2: A Quarto Interactive Presentation
section: 14.2 Load libraries and data once, near the top
text:
14.2 Load libraries and data once, near the top

library(tidyverse)
library(plotly)
library(gt)
library(DT)

static_file <- read_csv("data/static_file_sample.csv")

include: false hides both the code and any output from this setup chunk — appropriate here since loading libraries and data isn’t something your audience needs to see, only something the rest of the document depends on.
crumbs: 14  Project Walkthrough 2: A Quarto Interactive Presentation

title: 14  Project Walkthrough 2: A Quarto Interactive Presentation
section: 14.3 A slide with an interactive visualization
text: 14.3 A slide with an interactive visualization
crumbs: 14  Project Walkthrough 2: A Quarto Interactive Presentation

title: 14  Project Walkthrough 2: A Quarto Interactive Presentation
text:
14.4 Scale Score Distribution by Subject

p <- static_file |>
  filter(!is.na(ScaleScore)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = ScaleScore, fill = Subject)) +
  geom_histogram(alpha = 0.7, position = "identity") +
  labs(x = "Scale Score", y = "Number of Students") +
  theme_minimal()

ggplotly(p)

Because this is wrapped in ggplotly() (from Visualization), anyone viewing the rendered slide can hover over bars for exact values, zoom into a score range, or toggle subjects on and off — interactivity a static image in a PDF simply can’t offer.
crumbs: 14  Project Walkthrough 2: A Quarto Interactive Presentation

title: 14  Project Walkthrough 2: A Quarto Interactive Presentation
section: 14.5 A slide with an interactive table
text: 14.5 A slide with an interactive table
crumbs: 14  Project Walkthrough 2: A Quarto Interactive Presentation

title: 14  Project Walkthrough 2: A Quarto Interactive Presentation
crumbs: 14  Project Walkthrough 2: A Quarto Interactive Presentation

title: 14  Project Walkthrough 2: A Quarto Interactive Presentation
section: 14.7 Using inline variables in your narrative
text:
14.7 Using inline variables in your narrative
This is where Quarto’s literate-programming approach (text and code woven together, from Quarto Essentials) does real work in a presentation context. Rather than typing a number that will quietly go stale the next time this file is regenerated against updated data:
{r} 1+1


[1] "`r 1+1`"


Every number in that sentence is computed live from static_file at render time. Re-run this same document against next year’s data, and every figure updates correctly with zero manual editing — directly delivering on the reproducibility argument that’s been a throughline of this entire book.
crumbs: 14  Project Walkthrough 2: A Quarto Interactive Presentation

title: 14  Project Walkthrough 2: A Quarto Interactive Presentation
text:
14.8 Previewing and sharing
Use Preview (Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + K, from Quarto Essentials) while you build, to check slides as you go. When you’re ready to share:

Click Preview (or run quarto render from the terminal) to produce the final self-contained .html file
Click the pop-out button in the Viewer pane to open it in a full browser window, or simply double-click the rendered .html file from your file system — since embed-resources: true is set, it will open correctly anywhere, with no separate files required
Share that single .html file directly — email, shared drive, or anywhere else — and it will render identically on the recipient’s end
crumbs: 14  Project Walkthrough 2: A Quarto Interactive Presentation

title: 14  Project Walkthrough 2: A Quarto Interactive Presentation
text:
14.9 What you’ve actually built
A presentation that’s simultaneously a finished deliverable and a script — one that can be re-rendered against next year’s Static File with zero manual rework, that updates its own numbers automatically, and that gives your audience a genuinely interactive way to explore the underlying data rather than a static snapshot. This is, in miniature, the entire case for moving this kind of work into R: the investment is real, but it’s a one-time investment that pays out every single time you run it again.
crumbs: 14  Project Walkthrough 2: A Quarto Interactive Presentation

title: 15  Where to Go From Here
text:
15.1 A realistic next few weeks
You now have a working path from spreadsheets and SAS scripts to a real, reproducible R workflow — using data shaped like the work you actually do. A few honest closing notes, and where to turn when you’re past what this book covers.
You are not trying to become a software engineer. You’re trying to stop re-doing the same manual process every reporting cycle — and everything in this book was selected with that specific goal in mind.
crumbs: 15  Where to Go From Here

title: 15  Where to Go From Here
text:
Week 1: Re-run the chapters in Part 2 and the early part of Part 3 against a file from your own work, not just the Static File sample. Seeing your own data behave the way the book’s examples did is usually the moment this starts to feel real rather than theoretical.
Week 2: Pick one recurring task you currently do by hand — a pivot table you rebuild every reporting cycle, a VLOOKUP-heavy merge, a chart you reformat from scratch each time — and rebuild it once in R using this book’s recipes. Compare the output number-for-number against your existing process before trusting it.
Week 3 and beyond: Start saving your work as scripts or Quarto documents instead of redoing the same clicks next month. The first time you re-run something instead of repeating two hours of manual work is usually the moment the investment pays for itself.
crumbs: 15  Where to Go From Here

title: 15  Where to Go From Here
section: 15.2 Where to look when you’re stuck
text:
15.2 Where to look when you’re stuck

?function_name in the R console pulls up that function’s documentation directly — e.g., ?left_join
R for Data Science (free online, by Hadley Wickham & Garrett Grolemund) — the closest thing to a canonical tidyverse textbook: https://r4ds.hadley.nz
Posit Cheatsheets — printable quick-reference cards for dplyr, ggplot2, and more: https://posit.co/resources/cheatsheets/
Stack Overflow / Google — searching “r how to [task]” is a completely normal and permanent part of the workflow, not a beginner habit you’ll outgrow
The SGP package documentation and community — given the shared-language argument from Why R First, this is genuinely worth bookmarking once you’re ready to go deeper into growth-model-specific R tooling
crumbs: 15  Where to Go From Here

title: 15  Where to Go From Here
section: 15.3 A note on what this book didn’t cover
text:
15.3 A note on what this book didn’t cover
This book deliberately stayed narrow — a fast on-ramp, not a comprehensive data science curriculum. A few things worth knowing exist, even though they weren’t covered here: writing your own custom functions, R’s object-oriented programming patterns (relevant if you ever work directly in a larger inherited codebase), formal package development, and statistical modeling beyond simple summary statistics. None of those are necessary to be genuinely productive day to day — they’re worth exploring once the core workflow in this book feels comfortable, not before.
crumbs: 15  Where to Go From Here

title: 15  Where to Go From Here
text:
15.4 Last thing
You will look up basic syntax for months. Everyone who’s gone through this transition does — it is not a sign that you’re behind, and it never fully stops, even for experienced users. The goal was never to memorize everything in this book. It was to get you to the point where the first real script you write saves you real time — and from there, the rest tends to take care of itself.
crumbs: 15  Where to Go From Here

title: Appendix: Practice Ideas — Working With Public Files
text:
Idea 1: Clean and standardize a state-published Excel report
Once the core chapters feel comfortable, the best way to solidify the skills in this book is practicing on real, publicly available education data rather than only the Static File sample. A few specific ideas, roughly ordered from most to least guided.
Most state education agencies publish A–F results, report card data, or assessment summaries as multi-tab Excel workbooks intended for human reading — merged header cells, inconsistent column names across tabs, footnote rows mixed into the data, multiple title rows before the actual header. Download one publicly posted report from your own state’s website and put Common Functions and Importing From Everywhere to direct use:
This is the single most directly useful exercise, because it’s the exact category of messy, real-world file you’ll keep encountering professionally.
crumbs: Appendix: Practice Ideas — Working With Public Files

title: Appendix: Practice Ideas — Working With Public Files
text:
Use read_excel(..., skip = n) to skip past title rows that aren’t real headers
Run janitor::clean_names() immediately, and expect it to need a follow-up rename() pass for anything it can’t fully standardize on its own
Use the brute-force bind_rows() approach from Importing From Everywhere across every tab, then revisit with map_dfr() once you understand what you’re combining
Produce a short validation summary (row counts per tab, missing-value counts per column) as practice for the pattern from Project Walkthrough 1
crumbs: Appendix: Practice Ideas — Working With Public Files

title: Appendix: Practice Ideas — Working With Public Files
section: Idea 2: Build a multi-year trend file from separately published annual files
text:
Idea 2: Build a multi-year trend file from separately published annual files
Many states publish one separate file per year rather than one combined multi-year file. Download three or four years of the same report (say, statewide proficiency rates by subject) and practice:

Reading each year’s file separately, tagging each with its year before combining (the same source_sheet-style tagging pattern from Project Walkthrough 1)
Using bind_rows() or map_dfr() to combine them into one long-format trend dataset
Building a ggplot2 line chart (from Visualization) showing the trend over time, faceted by subject or school type

This is good practice specifically for the reshaping mindset in Reshaping — you’ll likely find that what looks like “the same report” each year occasionally has slightly different column names or structures year to year, which is itself a useful, realistic problem to solve.
crumbs: Appendix: Practice Ideas — Working With Public Files

title: Appendix: Practice Ideas — Working With Public Files
section: Idea 3: Compare your own state’s public file structure to another state’s
text:
Idea 3: Compare your own state’s public file structure to another state’s
If you work in one state’s accountability system, pulling a public assessment or accountability file from a different state is a good way to practice general-purpose cleaning instincts rather than ones already tailored to a file structure you know well. Look for:

Different column naming conventions for conceptually similar fields (your Performance may be another state’s AchievementLevel)
Different missing-data conventions (some states use blank cells, others use -1, N/A as literal text, or * for suppressed small-n cells)

Practice writing a case_when() or rename() mapping that translates the unfamiliar file’s structure into something matching your own Static File’s conventions — a genuinely useful skill if your role ever involves cross-state comparisons or multi-state research.
crumbs: Appendix: Practice Ideas — Working With Public Files

title: Appendix: Practice Ideas — Working With Public Files
section: Idea 4: Recreate a public-facing dashboard chart
text:
Idea 4: Recreate a public-facing dashboard chart
Most state report card or accountability websites have at least one public-facing chart or table (a proficiency-by-subgroup bar chart, a multi-year trend line, a school comparison table). Pick one, find or approximate the underlying public data, and try to recreate it using ggplot2 and gt from Visualization. This is good practice specifically because you have a known target to match — it’s much easier to tell whether your chart is “right” when you’re matching something that already exists, rather than building from scratch with no reference point.
crumbs: Appendix: Practice Ideas — Working With Public Files

title: Appendix: Practice Ideas — Working With Public Files
section: A general note on all of these
text:
A general note on all of these
Public education data files are excellent practice material precisely because they’re real and imperfect — inconsistent headers, surprise merged cells, footnote text mixed into data rows, and the occasional genuinely confusing column. That friction is exactly what this book intends to prepare you for. The Static File sample used throughout the rest of this book was deliberately realistic, but it was also clean enough to teach from without fighting the file itself on every example — these exercises are where you get the practice of fighting the file, which is a normal and permanent part of the job.
crumbs: Appendix: Practice Ideas — Working With Public Files
